I didn't plan to take the seminars. And there's a couple of reasons I'm bringing it. I'm going to tell you why. First reason is that there's a lot of people who just literally beg us to do it and they can't take the time off to work together. The second reason is that I believe that you have to get information. I've read 17 different times before you've really found that information. You need to get that information on an audio level. You get it on a video level. You get it on a tactile level. So we are going to be going to take this. We've done more professional than we've ever done before. We're going to do it with twin cameras. We're going to edit it very carefully. My old buddy Brock open to the west is going to a audio table. We're going to have some inserts that go with the video tapes and with the audio tapes. And I'm going to add some other material. That's going to be a product to me. And that product is going to sell for $2,700 the same price as the cinema. There is a special deal for everybody in this room and that deal is to get it free. However, that is financially free. Now, if you don't do what I tell you to do, which does not involve money, you don't get it. We all clear on that. And what I'm going to have to do in Walt's writing, one of the biggest things I have found when I'm teaching people to do things is they don't do it. Somehow they think they can understand things without doing it. It's like a man trying to believe that he understands childbirth. They never really do it. I have a friend who is arguably the deadliest man in the world in hand to hand combat. He doesn't teach very many people how to fight. He doesn't teach very many people how to fight. But those he does at the end of the class, he takes them out to microbars. They never have to pick any fights. The bikers take care of that. Now, if you learn hand-to-hand fighting from other people, you'll get rewarded with a black belt. And you will have to learn how to bow and you'll learn the history of the art and all the courtesies and everything. But for my money, if you have to bet on somebody winning the fight, I'm going to bet on the guy who's been out in the biker bars. And who has been kicking ass the last three to four years on the line. Now, I'm about to take you into the biker bar of copyright. I've been through this room responsible for the mailing of three and a half billion successful sales letters. That means that we're going to do it my way. And doing it my way, we may as well get started right now. So, to begin this seminar, I would like to tell you the following. My name is Gary Halbert. I'm the best copyrighter you've ever lived. In the next few days, I'm going to teach you how to do it. I'm not going to take any prisoners and I'm not going to take any shit from that. But if you pick up the envelope, that's by your table right there. And you pick up the envelope. You can't simply lose the envelope. This is a new envelope. Now, envelopes have a return address and on them. And that's where you write your name. I'll show you all right right there. Anybody need help with that? And below that, I want you to write Sol Miami Beach Hotel. Let's s-o-l Miami Beach Hotel. 39.5. Is that right? College Avenue. Miami Beach. Florida. And the zip, I think, is 3-3-1-4-0. Now, that should all be written right there. In direct mail, when we talk to professionals, they don't call that a return address. They call it a corner card just so that you know you have just written your corner card. Now, this corner card is going to be a little bit unusual because I want you to drop down. And I want you to write kind of in small. You know, there, I want you to write Friday. You can write in this for me. Friday. And then I want you to write today's date, which is 19-4-19-96. And below that, I want you to write the time, which is exactly by my watch, 10-1-8-9. Now, in the middle of the envelope is where we address the envelope. And we're going to address this envelope to someone special. How many of you in the room have a mother that's living? And I said, yes. How many of you have the rest of you have over at one time? Of course, if you have. Do you all remember her name? Anybody can't remember their mother's name? Then what I want you to do here, we're going to write a letter, hide it to your mother or to the member of your mother. That's what they do. I want you to write her name right there. Now, write in here on. Now, do you know how that goes? Circle slash little circle. That means in here on. And then write your name below that. And then write your street address. And then write your city, state, and zip below that. Okay. Now, you should have a stamp right there. Take that stamp, put it where the stamp goes, which is on the upper right hand corner of the envelope. That's the letter. Here is where are the letters that I have? Are they all in this folder? Mom. I have it here. Okay, now, some of you, we're going to write either Dear Mom or some of you have a special name for your mother or you did when she was alive. My father, my mom, my mom, chose to write Dear Mom and go with her mom. So, right back to your salutation, Dear Mom or her. Okay, now, we're going to write the first paragraph. There are a lot of ways to write letters. A lot of them aren't good. There is no way in three days I can teach you every way to write a letter, so I'm going to teach you one very good way. And then we can do Mom's shoot. I like to indent my paragraphs five spaces. So, we are now about to write the first sentence of the first paragraph. So, although you're handwriting it, I want you to know that if you were typing it, it would be indented five spaces. I'm baller than Dear Mom, so I want you to write. I'm very busy right now. And I don't have time to call. So, that's why I'm writing you this letter. That's your first paragraph. I don't always do it, but it's a good idea when you can to make your first paragraph one sentence. I don't know if that's not a rule of it. It's a good idea. A lot of times. So, now that's one sentence and it's one paragraph. So, I want you to drop down. I like to put space between my paragraphs. It makes it really easy for me to read. In depth five spaces, you know, comma, mom. Not only are you responsible. For my very existence. You are also the nourishment that has made my life sparkle, period. Here, same paragraph, that was sentence. I wonder a lot of stress right now. And for some reason and comma, for some reason, comma. I started thinking about you. Period. Same paragraph, new sentence. Maybe. I haven't let you know recently. How much I appreciate you. And how much I love you. Now, drop down to the new paragraph. Now, most of you have a memory of your mother at a birthday party. Well, when she came to your graduation, you were baked in a cake. And I want you to not write what I'm about to dictate, but write something personal. The new paragraph I have is, I especially like remembering you in that pink flower dress before at my 15th birthday. Now, I want you to write this new paragraph. I especially like remembering you. There's something I surely especially like remembering. New paragraph. Anyway, I'm going to make it a point to see you just as soon as I can. Period. Now we're going to do the salutation. And unless it's a formal letter, salutations go on the lower right. If it's a sales letter, I usually write sincerely, but this is a letter to your mother. So I want you to write love or all my love or whatever. And then write your name or any pet name that your mother has for you. Sign the letter. Fold it up. No p.s. this time. Put it in the envelope. It's a letter. It's a letter. And put it on that. I would put that on the envelope. I'd go to the Bahamas every once in awhile mentally. Actually, I feel there are a lot. OK, now we're going to the letter. Now I want you to write dear mom, no special names, and put quotes around the word mom, quotes before the first M, quote marks after the second M, and then put a colon instead of a comma. Then we're going to drop down to the first paragraph, which means that if we were typing it, we would indent five spaces. If I were such a long way from home, comma, I probably wouldn't have the guts to tell you this. Period. But comma, the following has been true ever since I was born. Period. I like to do, now we're going to center this below the paragraphs. I like to make subheads first caps. That means the first letter in the word is cap, and then the first letter in the second word is cap, and so forth. And if we were typing it, I'd like to make it bold. Here's your subhead. I have no inclination whatsoever. To communicate with you ever again, period. Then sign it reluctantly, your son or daughter. Whatever it happens to be, sign your name. We're going to write p.s. p.s. Dad sucks too. Fold those up, put them in the envelope and seal them. Well, he'll get the point. Okay, now if you turn around, you'll see this woman in the red coat with the pretty blue eyes, Audrey. See this woman right there? She's going to show you where the mailbox is. Go mail both those letters. Come back and we'll start the seminar. By the way, if your mother lives with you, there's something to make sure she doesn't get the letter. Okay, I can understand that. Just keep it with you. It's just to make a point. Now, Audrey, listen up. Sit down and shut up, Jean. You disrupt every seminar I have. There is a reason why I do everything I do, why I will do everything that I do in this seminar. I will not explain that. I will not stop to explain it while I'm doing it, but afterwards I do want to explain it because I want you to all understand this. Does anybody here think that that was a silly or empty exercise we just did? Okay, now I want to drive home some points about that. I'm going to ask some questions that may seem idiotic, but we need to drive some very fundamental truths home. I want to tell you why those truths need to be driven home. I don't know if any of you scuba dive, but if you do, you know that it is not a very complicated thing to do. There really is not a lot of things that you have to learn to scuba dive, but the few things that you do have to learn are crucial. Now, one of those things, for example, is that you don't hold your breath when you're scuba diving. Now, here's why. If you don't have scuba equipment on and you dive off a platform down into the water, you'll take a deep breath, and while you're on the surface, your lungs will expand to hold that air. And as you go down, the water pressure will contract your lungs, and they will become smaller and smaller and smaller. As you begin to ascend, your lungs will open up, and when you get to the surface, they will be the same size as they were when you dove in, all the time. If you are scuba diving and you are underwater already, let's say you're 60 feet down, and you take a breath, your lung, you will keep in taking oxygen until your lungs expand to say it as you always do, until they were the same size as they were when you took the breath on the surface, all right? And, but let's suppose for some reason, you decided then to ascend while holding your breath. Now, remember, your lungs are already expanded to their full capacity. As for the water pressure? The water pressure decreases, and your lungs, the air gets expand and expands and expands until your lungs will not expand anymore, so the air goes outside, it goes through your lungs, into your bloodstream, forms little bubbles, and it dripples like a dripple, and you go to your life, and you go to your life, and you go to your life. So, let's suppose that I were scuba diving stuff, and I said, first of all, scuba diving is never holding your breath. Now, let's go on with something else. Well, it could be that one day you could go underwater, and say, wow, I wonder what they're doing up there, and it's like something, you know, they're having a party on the boat, I think I'll get up there and take a deep breath, and hold it and shoot it in the surface. You were told that you shouldn't do that, and you got it in the lecture. But in this case, it's so important, is it not, that you have to get it on a cellar above, because it's a cellar above? Now, I'm going to teach you very, very sophisticated, complex things. But the more important things that I have to teach you are very simple, because that's where everybody goes bad, is on fundamental. People do not die scuba diving, because they were eaten by sharks, attacked by octopuses, or what have you. You know how they die? They die from holding their breath, or they drown, because they run out of air. And my first job here is to make sure that you get the fundamentals right. Now, I want to ask a question to the lady in the blue blazer. The love letter that you wrote to your mother, the postage that you put on it, how much was that postage stamp cost? 32 cents. And the oral hygienist over here. The love letter that you wrote to your mother. How much was the postage? Okay. How much difference in time, this fellow with the ponytail and the bonker, what's your name? Joe. Joe, how much more time did it take you to address the hate letter, as opposed to address the love letter? About the same? Okay. How much time did it take you, difference in time, was there to write the hate letter versus the love letter? No difference. No difference at all. I'd like to see a show of hands, of those of you who think, if your mother really received these letters, that there would be a dramatic difference in response from one letter to the other. Can I see your hands? You all believe that? Now, isn't that curious? The postage was the same. The envelope was the same. The amount of work required was the same. The letter shot work, which you did, letter shot, by the way, is the term for folding and inserting the envelope and sealing it, putting the address on and stamping it. It was the same. The only difference is the words on the piece of paper. That's the only difference. And words truly are the most powerful thing in the world. You can use words to wound. You can devastate people with your words. You can use words to encourage and to pump people up. You can use words to make you a fortune. You can use words, and I have done this, that'll cause you to get lost and lose your fortune. But the power is in what you write. Now, there's a lot of people, nobody hardly ever does it anymore because they're afraid to do it with me. But it used to be at my seminars. The first question I would get is, what kind of word processor or computer do you think I should use? And then I'd just go ballistic, you know? And all of these things are technical things. And I'm going to teach you, what I'm going to teach you here is what you really need to know. Now, every year, maybe once or twice or three times a year, you should do what you just did. You should hand write a letter. You should hand write addresses. You should get peel and stick labels. You should put them on envelopes. You should apply the stamps yourself. You should fold the letters. You should put them in the envelopes. And you should take them to the post office. You know, the last seminar that I did of this nature, in fact, the only other one, and this will be the last one for a long time, I got a letter from a woman by fax as soon as she got off the plane, or not quite as soon as she got off the plane, actually, it was when she got back to her office because she had a little bit of delay in going home. And she said, everything you taught us at that seminar came home to me when I went there and those two letters to my mom were on my desk because it was a reminder. Now, will or will you not remember those letters for the rest of your life? Will you remember them, ma'am? You're mad at me, aren't you? Oh, you're not? Okay. That means I'm a failure because... You know... Tell us a little bit about somebody else's matter. Every once in a while, I get a letter from a subscriber who's upset because I'm not raw enough in my letters, literally they are. And I noticed I hadn't been getting enough letters like that, complaining, hey, don't you think you're over the edge with your language and blah, blah, blah. So I started publishing my little tacky Mickey Mouse calendar, which has really caught on, it's become famous. You all get the little calendar, right? And I have a girlfriend, an ex-girlfriend in California, and I asked her what she thought of the most recent newsletter. And she said, I didn't read it. She said, I've known you, we've been together in one way or another for more than ten years, and that's the first newsletter of yours that I didn't read. And I said, why? She said, because I opened it up and the calendar fell out, and there was this girl holding herself, and I thought, you are over the line and that's it, you're gone too far. And I said, thank you, Loretta, thank you, my God. I hadn't outraged anybody so long. I'm starting to feel like Howard Stern, you know, telling toilet jokes that nobody is objecting. There's actually a point to that because I do not publish the calendar or write the letter to offend anybody, but there is a larger point that you need to understand here, and you need to begin writing some notes now. All right? Here is what contributes to whether or not people remember something. Recency. Everything else being equal. You will remember that which has happened recently. The second thing that contributes to memory is frequency. Everything else being equal, you will remember something that has happened repetitively over and over. The third thing that contributes to memory is vividness. In other words, how impactful it was. Now, had I told you from a professorial standpoint that the words on paper could make a difference, a big difference, it wouldn't have had nearly the vividness of what I just did, would it? And the fact that I made you get up and go drop it in the mail is going to contribute to your remembering it. The fact that it's going to be waiting for you when you get home is going to help you remember it. We are all of us exposed. The numbers vary, but the point is the same. To about 1,500, 1,700 sales messages a day, commercial messages. And we don't remember most of them. As a point of survival, we could not. So a lot of times when people are selling something and someone else is selling something very similar, for example, let's say you're selling washing machines. Granted, there are differences between brands, but basically a washing machine is a washing machine. If I were to write you a letter about a washing machine, how would I make it? What would I want to do? And I would want to make my letter vivid. Not only does it need to be vivid and have impact when it is opposed to all the other letters about washing machines that you may happen to get in that period of time, it needs to have impact competitively against every other thing that's competing for your attention in that life, in your life at that period of time. If it doesn't, it's not going to bring back any money for you. If you do not make an impression, if you do not make any impact, then nobody's going to remember your message. It has been suggested to me over the years by some well-meaning people that I tone down my newsletter. And if I had done that, I wouldn't have received as many bitch letters as I have. But also, 60% of my subscribers would not be lifers, and I wouldn't have the renewals that I have. What you want to remember is that you are going after the buyers. Now, I believe that you should not attempt to deliberately offend anybody. But the first thing you should learn about writing is that almost everybody who writes thinks that they are writing too emotionally. And the truth is, you're not writing emotionally or impactfully enough. What helps, I think, is for you to remember, or to have it illustrated for you, what we're playing for here. I'm going to use this pad, and I want to see if I can fix it so that everybody can see what I'm about to write here. I'm going to show you the idea of the difference between getting it right and not getting it right. I'm going to try and dramatize that for you so that you'll really be motivated to understand why you should get what I'm talking to you about. Let's pick a targeted audience. The audience that I always like to pick is the audience of people who need to lose weight, because that's a very large audience. Not only people who need to lose weight, but who want to lose weight, and people who have evidenced that desire by buying something, to help them lose weight, a diet pill, a tummy flattener, low-calorie frozen foods, or what have you. And preferably, they will have purchased such a thing through the mail. Now, I can tell you that if you were to research this through a good list broker, you would find out that there are about three million hardcore names of mail-order diet buyers. Now, this illustration, I want to pause for a moment to talk about the difference between associative thinkers and linear thinkers. Neither of them are superior to the other. It's just that they're different, and the world needs both of them. But as much as you can, if you're not already, you need to become an associative thinker. An associative thinker will understand the association from the example I'm going to give you on weight loss to the example on other areas. It's the principle that I'm trying to illustrate here. Now, a linear thinker, I have had seminars and guys stand up and say, I own an orchard and I grow oranges. Explain to me a marketing plan how to sell them, and I do. And the next guy will raise his hand and say, I'm an orchard business too, but I grow apples. What's that got to do with me? That's a linear thinker. Computer nerds, for example, are linear thinkers. You need to begin to think associatively. Now, let's say we have three million people who have bought something by mail that is related to losing weight. Now, let's suppose that you have something that will help them lose weight. And we're going to make this something for the purposes of this example, an information product instead of an ingestible. What we're going to do in our minds, let's say that we have gone to the library and gone to a couple of bookstores, and we have gotten 10 or 15 books on how to lose weight. We have extracted all the good ideas from there. We have added our own ideas, and we have created a well-written, really valuable report on how to lose weight, let's say 60 pages long. And not only does it extract information from those books, you put in there things that help you lose weight, that you know are real. You make it personal. So now you have a 60-page report. And I can always think better if I make little drawings. And here's our little 60-page report, OK? Now, do you do people a favor when you give them crucial information through as many sensory channels as possible? So what you do with this report is you read it into a microphone. And you create, by reading that into the microphone, a little set of six cassettes. Like they have little holes in them, OK? And to make a further impact on the person and convince them of what you're doing, you have a videotape that you made. And let's suppose that back in the days when you were overweight yourself, you figured out a way that you thought would help you, and you videotaped it, and you videotaped your progress, and now you weigh what you want to weigh, so now you include a videotape here, OK? Now, some of you know the answer to the next question better than I do. This is by videotape. And we put all of this in a nice package, OK? A little box, and they open it, and there's the report, you know, it's like this thick, and on top of that are the six tapes, and here's the video tape. And we put a nice case in it. Let's talk about how much that cost. Some of you know, does anybody give me a good guess on how much the 60-page report's going to cost to print? Somebody here must have a clue, huh? 90 cents? OK, that might be a little high, but let's make it a dollar. Anybody in here know anything about audio tapes? How much do you think it would cost to make the six audio tapes? $3 apiece or for all six? For all six. Anybody here know what it would take to do, cost to do the videotape? Two bucks. OK. Now, how about the case to put it in? Well, these little plastic cases that will hold everything? Somebody give me a clue, huh? Two bucks? OK. Now, that's our fulfillment package cost, right? $1 plus 3 is 4 plus 2 is 5 plus 2 is 7. We haven't mailed it yet, though. I knew that. I was just checking to see if you could catch that. $8, right? OK. Now, we have to ship this out to the people. How much is it going to cost us to ship it? Somebody in this room knows probably, huh? Three bucks? So what are we at? $11? OK. And we have a package here of pretty high perceived value. Do we not? With a fulfillment cost of $11. Now, I'm looking around. I don't have any of you, I don't see very, except for Gene Dove. I don't see any porters in the room. Have any of you ever had a weight problem at all? Can I see your hand? Weight problem? OK. Did you have difficulty overcoming that weight problem? OK. Now, I want to see if you know the answer to that, because maybe you wouldn't. But what do you think a person who is 50 pounds overweight would pay just for a magic pill that would make them act their proper weight for the rest of their life with no negative effects on them? That would be, what do you think a person would pay? Gene, what do you think somebody would pay? How much? A couple hundred? Now, remember what I'm talking about. I'm talking about this is not true. There is no such thing. But suppose there were one magic pill. You took it once. You would never have a weight problem again. The weight would go away. What do you think? About six months' pay. About six months' pay. OK. Anybody else have an opinion of what someone would pay? Everything they would afford. You got it. Everything they would afford. They would sell their house, sell their car. If it were true, and they believed it, they'd pay everything. They would. You know, so what I'm, the point I want to make here though is when they decide whether or not to buy this, how much consideration do they give to how much you pay to print the reports and do the audio and video tapes. None whatsoever. There's only one thing they care about. Will that work? OK. Now, let us suppose that your letter convinces them that what you have for them will work. And so we price it at $69.95 plus $4.00 shipping and handling. That's a good price point, by the way. We're going to round that off to $74. That's the price. OK. But price is not profit, so we have to subtract the cost of filling that order. We have $63.00, and I want you to write this term down because it's something you really need to know if you're going to entrepreneur any of these things. CTO. You have $63.00 contribution to overhead. That's not $63.00 profit. That's $63.00 over and above what it cost you to produce that. Suppose that the letter you just wrote to your mother, you wrote to her because you loved her and she had a wake problem and you were concerned about her health. And you were writing, say, Mom, I'm down here in Florida or I'm in San Diego. I've just talked to a doctor. I've discovered a new way to lose weight that really works and etc. Now, this beautiful reddit here. How much would the postage on that letter cost to mail it? $0.32.00. Would it cost the same as those letters you mailed to your mother? Let's suppose that that letter were four, six, eight pages. What do you think it would cost to print one of those letters? Anybody have a clue? Actually, it's closer to a nickel. One letter. Yeah, I mean just the printing of the letter. Let's say that the letter that you... We'll do it to your mom because your mom wouldn't need it. Like that letter that you just wrote, one page, one side. Let's suppose you had a type and now you're reproducing it. Okay? Because you're sending it to everybody's mother because you're the unabomber and you can't make bombs. The most that would cost you to reproduce... I mean, you could Xerox them for two cents, right? We're going to make it a multi-page letter. We're going to say, now this is the postage. This is the printing of the letter. How much do you think the envelope would cost that you just used there? I mean, I don't know, but the answer is it doesn't matter really too much, does it? I mean three cents, can we say three cents? The point is if I come out with a number that anybody wants to challenge, that's fine because we'll adjust it. But let's say it's three cents and to make it easy for your mom, you put in another envelope with the address of the doctor on it. Let's say that costs you three cents too. And let's suppose that some of you, being incredibly dense, can't remember who your mother is or where she lived, so you had to go to a mailing list broker and say, I want my mother's name. How much do you think it would cost her name to get her name? A list of mothers, what we're talking about. How much? I think that's a little high. Sometimes they are, sometimes they're less. Let's make it a dime, okay? A hundred bucks, you know, a thousand. You could buy names for a nickel a piece, six cents a piece, eight cents, ten cents, twelve cents, fifteen cents, twenty cents. We're going to list them first. And actually, that's a bad example, it's lost its relevance when I went to the list. Let's say it's a list of people who buy mail order stuff, okay? About ten cents. Now, this is the postage, this is the letter, this is the two envelopes, and this is the name that you're going to mail it to. Now, we also have something called letter shop. And letter shop is all the mechanical things that have to be done to make that letter go out. You have to print, well, not print, it's not printing, but it's the folding, it's the inserting, it's the sealing, it's the fixing of a stamp or metering or whatever they do to put the address on the labeling, etc. Does anybody know, can give me an idea of what, Jean, you know what letter shop is? Yeah. Jean Dahl, by the way, is one of the best copywriters in the country and an excellent person to know if you're going to be in the direct mail business. You say fifteen cents? Fifteen, right? What if you don't? Make it a dime because it's for easy stuff here, okay? Thirty-two, thirty-seven, and three is forty, forty-three, fifty-three, sixty-three. That means you could mail those letters for sixty-three cents a piece. Now, let me talk to you about that. For a long time, I have said that I believe that you should always mail first-class mail. I still believe that to be true in most of the cases, but in reality, what I believe a hundred percent is that you should mail letters that look like they're first-class mail. And you can achieve that with a postage meter. And you can get your, what's the lowest balk rate, Jean? About fifteen cents. So you can get this down, you can knock fifteen cents off this by mailing balk rate, okay? So that goes down to, what, forty-eight cents or something. You can't do it like that. Now, let me tell you what the reality is. This is an example that's not dead on, but I'm going to tell you what the reality is because we only need to be close enough for me to make my points, okay? The reality is, is that you can mail sales letters for an average of somewhere between thirty-five cents a letter and sixty-five cents a letter, day in and day out. Now, when you get down into the low prices, the really thirty-five cents a letter or something, you're talking about people who mail hundreds of millions of letters, you know, ten million a month, and they're getting the lowest price on everything. If you're talking about sixty-five cents, you're probably doing it low volume or doing something really special with it. But with a good, with a good average to think about is it's going to cost you about fifty cents to mail every sales letter you've mailed. Think of that as a rule of thumb. If it costs a little more, don't worry about it. If it costs a little less, rejoice. But we can think, can we not, about fifty cents to mail that letter to your mother, okay? Indirect mail. As you become versed in it and talk to the people in the business, you will find that everybody talks per thousand. How much, how much does it cost? They don't even say the end of it. You know, and they'll say fifty bucks. That means fifty dollars per thousand. And a lot of times they write that per M, capital M. So we're going to start talking in terms per thousand. So if our letter to the people who need to lose weight costs fifty cents apiece, what does it cost, obviously, to mail one thousand of those? Five hundred dollars. And that is the figure that I use unless there's something unusual about the mailing I'm doing to calculate the cost of putting out a mailing. I calculated five hundred bucks a thousand as a rule of thumb. Okay? Now, let's say that the letter that we did to describe our product is really, really good. And remember, we've got a sixty page report, six audio facets, one, you know, we've got a video tape. We're selling it for sixty nine ninety five plus four. Let's say that we're mailing it to really core lists. What do you think is a reasonable response that we could expect to get? Not outrageously good, not outrageously bad. What could we get? Something like that. Two percent? Two percent? Yeah, that's a pretty good thing. Two percent of a thousand. And please don't think I'm talking down to anybody because when I make mistakes, I make them on the kindergarten level. That's where everybody makes them, you know? Two percent of a thousand is how much? Twenty. Now, let's go back over our fulfillment cost. Our fulfillment is sixty nine ninety five, or our cost is sixty nine ninety five plus four. We're going to round that off to seventy four dollars and we deduct what for making a package and shipping it? Eleven. And that left us with what? Sixty three. Okay. Now, put the sixty three out here just for the moment, set that aside. Now, after the cost of fulfillment, we have sixty three dollars left over and we had to fill twenty orders. Okay? A thousand two hundred and sixty dollars. But we spent five hundred to get it. Now, remember this is already deducting the cost of product, but we still have to minus five hundred. Seven sixty. Per thousand. Right? Now, again, I'm not talking down to you. I would rather be fundamental than have important points missed. How much is that for a letter? Profit. No, for one letter. Seventy six cents profit a letter. Seven hundred and sixty dollars profit per thousand, right? You all know what a million is? A million is one thousand thousands. So, if there are three million core diet names, it means there are three thousand thousands. Does it not? Okay. This is what we are making per thousand and there are three thousand. Is that right? Two million two hundred and eighty thousand dollars profit. Counting what you would make on the back end, selling related products, not counting list rental. How far are you away from being able to do that? One letter. That's exactly how far you are away from being able to do that. Now, I'm going to teach you how to write that letter. When you walk out of here, you will know how to do that. And I am somebody who really knows how to do that. There have been six hundred million of these letters written, sent, mailed so far. And I wrote it in 1968. This or a very close adaptation to it. And they still mail thirty million of them a year. That's the Code of Arms letter. That's the Code of Arms.
